text: Oh, the causal arrow for acquiring rigor can certainly start with intuition and play, its just that I  don't see how you would get from that level of play to the point where you can solve hard problems. There is a gap between the intuition and rigor of a turtle mathland environment and what you do in calculus class. I like your example with the conch shell though. I'm picturing a turtle programatically running through some rectangular or triangular mesh of the region and adding up the volumes.

text:
It's an interesting question, and one potential perspective is to see symbolic calculus as just augmented thought. The mathematical rigor and semantics of the system allows new discovery, but that isn't fundamentally different than more physical proxies. Many concepts in calculus aren't fundamentally difficult either when you break it down, and there's no real reason that children/adults of all ages would not be able to understand "difficult" symbolic logic when piecewise, the pieces are quite simple.

Given this is the future of coding slack, I think we realize the importance of well crafted, composable abstractions that enable us to augment our thinking (albeit in the computational context, an executable form of thought). If children were given blocks that could form other blocks, then there really wouldn't be a real barrier to something like calculus. I think one argument is that the threshold is if those pieces and tools they have allow that kind of expansion - something like pencil and paper certainly meets that threshold.

text:
<@U01T2PC8LBA> I really like the perspective "symbolic calculus as augmented thought" and I wouldn't hesitate to read "calculus" in the broad sense either ("lambda calculus", "calculus raticionator"). Symbolic reasoning is awesome, but sometimes it has its shortcomings. A term I prefer is "formal reasoning". I'm not sure if a mathland for calculus absolutely needs symbols, but formal reasoning is a necessity.

By blocks do you just mean these abstractions you can compose with each other? Maybe you have a clearer idea how you could do this than me. On a gut level it seems possible, although I'm a bit puzzled then why we don't seem to have examples of work in this direction.

text:
(tl;dr: <http://worrydream.com/KillMath/>)

I think Papert would believe that the most important things to get from calculus are the underlying concepts: "measure the rate of change of one quantity relative to another" and "accumulate one quantity as another changes".

These concepts give you a jaw-droppingly powerful language for understanding and modeling and predicting the world. I mean this qualitatively, but a rigorous understanding of this language also leads to differential equations, which underly essentially all dynamic systems.

When you say "solve problems like the area under a cycloid or parabola", my guess is you're talking about symbolically computing derivatives & integrals. This seems to me to be vastly less important. (Especially symbolic integration! I learned so many integration tricks in high school which I have literally never used, even throughout an undergraduate education in math and physics.)

You might not mean symbolic computation, though! You might mean knowing:

1. how a shape can be broken down into small pieces [strips below the parabola],
2. how this can be described as a relationship between quantities [strip height = f(x)],
3. how integration expresses the assembly of these small pieces [area = ∫ strip height × strip width = ∫ f(x) dx]
4. how hand-calculation or a computer program could approximate this integral.

I'm into all of these things, and I expect Papert would be as well.

When you say "solve problems like the area under a cycloid or parabola", my guess is you're talking about symbolically computing derivatives & integrals. This seems to me to be vastly less important. (Especially symbolic integration! I learned so many integration tricks in high school which I have literally never used, even throughout an undergraduate education in math and physics.)

You might not mean symbolic computation, though! You might mean knowing:

1. how a shape can be broken down into small pieces [strips below the parabola],
2. how this can be described as a relationship between quantities [strip height = f(x)],
3. how integration expresses the assembly of these small pieces [area = ∫ strip height × strip width = ∫ f(x) dx]
4. how hand-calculation or a computer program could approximate this integral.

I'm into all of these things, and I expect Papert would be as well.

text:
So much to unpack.  Won't do it justice.

(1) Calculus as a class combines two types of ideas: (a) theory of limits, derivatives, integrals, (b) practice of fairly sophisticated formula manipulation.

(2) A Mathland focuses on giving students a feel for (a) theory.

(3) One can master the (b) practice without a good feel for how the manipulations are related to (a) theory. A lot of time energy spent on the product rule, not so much in getting a really good feel for what it does.

text:
> In a programmable space, the concept of a computer is expanded outside a little rectangular screen. Interacting with a programmable space means using physical objects, not virtual ones on a screen. Bringing computing to the scale of a room makes it a communal and social experience.
<https://programmable.space/>

text: :wave: That’s my project and I am occasionally on this Slack. Two years ago it started out as a Dynamicland clone but since then I have been playing and making demos in more ways that programming and the things people make can be made more physical.

text:
There are many examples of children intuiting their way to these things in Kay's writing. I can vouch for this phenomenon as I ended up reinventing calculus at around eight years old to write little simulation programs using 1970s era 8-bit computers.

Expanding on what <@UA14TGLTC> says above, not only can the common notation and formula manipulation tricks can be learnt with little or no real intuitive understanding, but likewise the intuition can be learnt quite well without that notation. I would even go on to say that in most regards an executable notation is superior to the 19th century standard.

<http://vpri.org/pdf/rn2007006a_olpc.pdf>

text: Could someone create a web browser or browser extension that just adds bidirectional links navigation, filtering, and querying to the web more broadly? Any reason that can’t just be layered on top of URLs already existing on the web?

elements.text: Could someone create a web browser or browser extension that just adds bidirectional links navigation, filtering, and querying to the web more broadly? Any reason that can’t just be layered on top of URLs already existing on the web?

text: Depending on exactly what you're asking for there's no reason other than inertia. For it to become useful you need a critical mass of people contributing to it.

elements.text: Depending on exactly what you're asking for there's no reason other than inertia. For it to become useful you need a critical mass of people contributing to it.

text:
Hmm. When I think of "mathland" I think being embedded in an environment where you learn math through play as its primary feature, and its capacity to communicate the main ideas of a subject, calculus for example, as secondary. I also think of the concept as being applicable to every level of math and physics. I learn these things by playing around with them, and I find that some environments are more conducive than others. But all of these environments are theoretical ones existing in other peoples heads that are communicated through the normal symbolic, verbal, and occasionally visual channels. So I'm admittedly coming from a bit broader a place though. I just can't think of any other image that communicates the idea of math through play better than mathland, so I'm appropriating the idea to a degree.

Zooming back in a little, the main "feature" thats missing is proofs or an equivalent. I really could have asked what class of mathlands have these things. And the whole discussion could be much simplified by just considering Euclidean geometry for instance. And when I was mentioning the cycloid and parabola I was thinking of these as an extension of euclidean geometry, as done by archimedes and others before calculus. No symbolic machinery needed, but its probably all the harder for it and not at all realistic.

Another direction it can go is a mathland supplanting symbolic methods. Kill Math already provides some vocabulary for this, and I'd venture to say those methods can be just as powerful as the symbolic ones. After all, any variable is just a number that varries, and a function is just a number that varies with another number.

text: The most obvious approach I can picture would just be taking URL links that already exist as backlinks. Why would that need a critical mass of people contributing to it?

elements.text: The most obvious approach I can picture would just be taking URL links that already exist as backlinks. Why would that need a critical mass of people contributing to it?

text:
Ah, got it, just turning existing links into backlinks, I had misunderstood you!

This is certainly possible, though there are some very real challenges!

• a bootstrap problem, if you care about links between different domains then it's always going to be incomplete, period, and it's probably going to miss the one important link you're looking for unless you're indexing most of the web. Indexing most of the web is quite expensive! So it'll be hard to get paying customers without first spending a lot of money.
• The web is mostly trash, so you'll need to put a lot of effort into filtering out the unimportant backlinks
• You'll probably never be able to index links from places like Facebook and Instagram, and it'll be very difficult to index links from places like Twitter. A large amount of activity goes on in there that you won't have access to!

text:
In a fundamental sense, it's not possible because linking is a one-way action. I can link to your website from an HTML file hosted off this laptop - how would you ever know?

In a practical sense you can probably get close because of how much tracking there is in the real internet. I would look into Google Analytics API for that. Or you could try some heuristic like the following Google search; `"<http://futureofcoding.org|futureofcoding.org>" -<site:futureofcoding.org>` which searches for a URL, excluding from that domain itself.

elements.text:
In a fundamental sense, it's not possible because linking is a one-way action. I can link to your website from an HTML file hosted off this laptop - how would you ever know?

In a practical sense you can probably get close because of how much tracking there is in the real internet. I would look into Google Analytics API for that. Or you could try some heuristic like the following Google search;
futureofcoding.org
which searches for a URL, excluding from that domain itself.

text: Google makes it very hard to look for links in this way because revealing too much about which links Google recognizes would greatly help spammers. I think to make anything useful you would have to do your own crawling, probably starting from an index like Common Crawl.

text:
:satellite_antenna: I have a new project to share with you: *<https://ivanish.ca/hest-podcast/|Hest — The Podcast>*

What's Hest? It's my own personal FoC project, and I'll be using this new podcast to explore the design of it in great depth. The podcast is inspired by early episodes of the Future of Coding podcast, in which Steve recapped his research with a style that felt to me a little like _daydreaming_. I enjoyed those episodes very much, and have wanted to do something similar ever since I took over Steve's show. I've come up with my own twist on the format that should make it even more enjoyable and useful. Episodes will be brief, frequent, and light. There are already 6 episodes in the feed, so that you can try it out for a bit to see if you enjoy it before deciding to subscribe on an ongoing basis. The primary audience is... myself — this is an audio sketchbook. But I think some of you will find it interesting too.

For a slightly longer writeup about the podcast: <https://ivanish.ca/hest-podcast/>

To subscribe,
• use this hand-crafted RSS feed: <https://ivanish.ca/hest.rss>
• search for *Hest* or *Ivan Reese* in your podcast player of choice,
• or click one of the buttons here: <https://pod.link/1559446316>
This might not appeal to many people, but for those to whom it does, I sincerely hope you enjoy it.

Backstage note: I'm writing the RSS by hand, as a learning experience. If you encounter any weirdness with this show in your podcast player, I'd love it if you DM'd me and let me know so I can try to fix it.

Beyond that, I welcome any and all feedback about the show, Hest itself, or anything else.
